content:
# Exodus Chapter 13 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~38v-39r (PDF pp. 75-76); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 53-54 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_013_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_013_page_002.jpg`

---

## Consecration of the Firstborn (vv. 1-2)

**13:1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: [fn1]

**13:2** "Consecrate to me every firstborn, the opener of every womb among the sons of Israel, among man and among beast -- it is mine." [fn2]

---

## The Observance of Unleavened Bread and the Teaching of Sons (vv. 3-10)

**13:3** And Moses said to the people, "Remember this day, on which you went out from Egypt, from the house of slaves, for by strength of hand YHWH brought you out from this place; and leavened bread shall not be eaten. [fn3]

**13:4** Today you are going out, in the month of the Aviv. [fn4]

**13:5** And it shall be, when YHWH brings you to the land of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Hivite and the Jebusite, which he swore to your fathers to give to you -- a land flowing with milk and honey -- then you shall keep this service in this month. [fn5]

**13:6** Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there shall be a feast to YHWH. [fn6]

**13:7** Unleavened bread shall be eaten for the seven days; and leavened bread shall not be seen with you, and leaven shall not be seen with you in all your territory. [fn7]

**13:8** And you shall tell your son on that day, saying, 'On account of this YHWH did for me when I went out from Egypt.' [fn8]

**13:9** And it shall be for you as a sign upon your hand and as a memorial between your eyes, so that the instruction of YHWH may be in your mouth; for by a strong hand YHWH brought you out from Egypt. [fn9]

**13:10** And you shall keep this statute at its appointed time, from year to year." [fn10]

---

## The Law of the Firstborn (vv. 11-16)

**13:11** "And it shall be, when YHWH brings you to the land of the Canaanite, as he swore to you and to your fathers, and gives it to you -- [fn11]

**13:12** then you shall set apart to YHWH every opener of the womb, and every firstling of the offspring of a beast that is yours -- the males shall be YHWH's. [fn12]

**13:13** And every firstling of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb; and if you do not redeem it, then you shall break its neck. And every firstborn of man among your sons you shall redeem. [fn13]

**13:14** And it shall be, when your son asks you tomorrow, saying, 'What is this?' -- then you shall say to him, 'By strength of hand YHWH brought us out from Egypt, from the house of slaves. [fn14]

**13:15** And it was, when Pharaoh was stubborn about sending us away, that YHWH killed every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of man to the firstborn of beast. Therefore I sacrifice to YHWH every opener of the womb that is male, and every firstborn of my sons I redeem.' [fn15]

**13:16** And it shall be as a sign upon your hand and as frontlets between your eyes, for by strength of hand YHWH brought us out from Egypt." [fn16]

---

## The Route Through the Wilderness (vv. 17-18)

**13:17** And it was, when Pharaoh sent the people away, that God did not lead them by the way of the land of the Philistines, though it was near; for God said, "Lest the people change their minds when they see war, and return to Egypt." [fn17]

**13:18** And God caused the people to go around by the way of the wilderness toward the Sea of Reeds. And the sons of Israel went up armed from the land of Egypt. [fn18]

---

## The Bones of Joseph (v. 19)

**13:19** And Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, for he had made the sons of Israel solemnly swear, saying, "God will surely visit you, and you shall bring up my bones from here with you." [fn19]

---

## The Journey to Etham -- Pillar of Cloud and Fire (vv. 20-22)

**13:20** And they journeyed from Succoth and encamped at Etham, at the edge of the wilderness. [fn20]

**13:21** And YHWH was going before them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead them on the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give light to them -- to go by day and by night. [fn21]

**13:22** **He did not remove the pillar of cloud by day or the pillar of fire by night from before the people.** [fn22]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- YHWH speaks to Moses alone** -- Hebrew: *vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor*. The verb *dibber* ("to speak") introduces a new legislative unit. Unlike 12:1, where both Moses and Aaron were addressed, this command is directed to Moses alone. The shift signals a new topic: the consecration of the firstborn and the observance of unleavened bread in the promised land. All three translators agree on the substance. EMET transliterates "Mosheh"; LOGOS uses "the LORD"; PNEUMA drops "And" and "saying." The consensus follows SOPHIA convention: YHWH transliterated, standard English "Moses," conjunction "And" preserved, "saying" retained. LXX: *eipen de kurios pros Mousēn legōn*.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Peter kol-rechem*: "the opener of every womb"** -- Hebrew: *qaddesh-li khol-bekhor peter kol-rechem bivnei Yisra'el ba'adam uvabbehemah li hu*. The imperative *qaddesh* (Piel of *qadash*, "to be holy") commands consecration -- the setting apart of the firstborn for divine ownership. The phrase *peter kol-rechem* ("opener of every womb") is the technical cultic term: *peter* (from *patar*, "to open, release, separate") denotes the first offspring that opens the birth canal. The firstborn is defined by the mother's womb, not the father's lineage. The emphatic *li hu* ("it is mine") asserts YHWH's claim: the firstborn, spared when Egypt's firstborn were struck, now belongs to YHWH. EMET renders "the opener of every rechem [womb]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the opener of every womb" formally. PNEUMA renders "the first to open every womb" dynamically. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "the opener of every womb" -- preserving the Hebrew's anatomical specificity through the noun *peter* rendered as "opener" rather than PNEUMA's adjectival "the first to open." LXX: *hagiason moi pan prōtotokon prōtogenes dianoigon pasan mētran* -- adding *prōtogenes* ("first-begotten") as a clarifying expansion.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Zakhor*, *beit avadim*, *be-khozeq yad*: "Remember," "house of slaves," "by strength of hand"** -- Hebrew: *vayomer Mosheh el-ha'am zakhor et-hayyom hazzeh asher yetsa'tem miMitsrayim mibbeit avadim ki be-khozeq yad hotsi YHWH etkhem mizzeh velo ye'akhel chamets*. The infinitive absolute *zakhor* functions as imperative: "Remember!" -- not passive recollection but active, liturgical re-enactment. The phrase *beit avadim* ("house of slaves") is the fixed epithet for Egypt (cf. Deut. 5:6; 6:12). The phrase *be-khozeq yad* ("by strength of hand") recurs four times in this chapter (vv. 3, 9, 14, 16) as the theological refrain of YHWH's deliverance. All three translators substantially agree. PNEUMA renders *beit avadim* as "house of slavery" -- the consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "house of slaves" -- preserving the concrete noun rather than the abstract. LXX: *en gar cheiri krataia exēgagen humas kurios* ("with a mighty hand the Lord brought you out").

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Ha'Aviv*: "the month of the Aviv"** -- Hebrew: *hayyom attem yots'im be-khodesh ha'Aviv*. The noun *Aviv* means "fresh, young ears of grain" -- the month when barley ripens, later called Nisan. The definite article *ha-* makes it a proper designation. EMET renders "the Aviv" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Aviv" as a proper name. PNEUMA renders "the month of Aviv" without the article. The consensus renders "the month of the Aviv" -- preserving the definite article present in the Hebrew (*ha'Aviv*), which marks this as a specific, named season. LXX: *en mēni tōn neōn* ("in the month of the new [grain]").

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Avodah*: "service" -- and the five nations** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah ki-yevi'akha YHWH el-erets ha-Kena'ani ve-ha-Chitti ve-ha'Emori ve-ha-Chivvi ve-ha-Yevusi asher nishba la'avotekha latet lakh erets zavat chalav u-devash ve'avadta et-ha'avodah hazzot ba-khodesh hazzeh*. Five nations are listed (contrast the fuller lists of six or seven elsewhere). The word *avodah* ("service, worship") -- the same root that described Egyptian slavery -- now describes sacred observance. EMET renders "avodah [service]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "service." PNEUMA renders "practice." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "service" -- preserving the theological resonance of *avodah* as both service and worship. PNEUMA's "practice" loses this double meaning. The phrase *erets zavat chalav u-devash* ("a land flowing with milk and honey") is rendered uniformly by all three translators. LXX adds the Gergesites to the MT's five peoples, making six.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- Seven days of unleavened bread** -- Hebrew: *shiv'at yamim tokhal matstsot u-va-yom ha-shevi'i khag laYHWH*. All three translators agree on the substance. The seventh day is a *khag* ("feast, festival") to YHWH. LXX reads *hex* ("six") for MT's *shiv'at* ("seven") -- a notable variant where LXX may reckon the seventh day as feast-only rather than a day of eating unleavened bread.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Chamets* and *se'or*: "leavened bread" and "leaven"** -- Hebrew: *matstsot ye'akhel et shiv'at ha-yamim velo-yera'eh lekha chamets velo-yera'eh lekha se'or bekhol-gevulekha*. Two distinct terms: *chamets* ("leavened bread," the product) and *se'or* ("leaven, sourdough starter," the fermenting agent). The verb *yera'eh* (niphal of *ra'ah*, "to see") -- "shall not be seen" -- establishes a stricter standard than mere prohibition of eating. EMET and LOGOS render with formal precision. PNEUMA renders *se'or* as "yeast" and *yera'eh* as "be found." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "leaven" for *se'or* (not "yeast," which is a modern substance) and "shall not be seen" for *yera'eh* (not "be found," which weakens the visual prohibition).

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ve-higgadta* and *ba'avur zeh*: "you shall tell" and "on account of this"** -- Hebrew: *ve-higgadta le-vinkha ba-yom ha-hu lemor ba'avur zeh asah YHWH li be-tse'ti miMitsrayim*. The verb *higgadta* (Hiphil of *nagad*, "to declare, tell") is the root of *Haggadah* -- the Passover telling. The phrase *ba'avur zeh* ("on account of this") is ambiguous by design: "this" may point forward (to the observance) or backward (to the event). The first-person *li* ("for me") collapses temporal distance -- each generation speaks as if they personally went out from Egypt. EMET renders "on account of this [ba'avur zeh] YHWH did [it] for me." LOGOS renders "it is because of what the LORD did for me." PNEUMA renders "this is because of what YHWH did for me." The consensus follows EMET's more literal reading: "on account of this YHWH did for me" -- preserving the Hebrew's ambiguous *ba'avur zeh* and its emphatic fronting, rather than resolving the ambiguity as LOGOS/PNEUMA do. LXX: *dia touto epoiēsen kurios ho theos moi* ("because of this the Lord God did for me").

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Ot*, *zikkaron*, and the tefillin connection** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah lekha le'ot al-yadkha u-le-zikkaron bein einekha lema'an tihyeh torat YHWH be-fikha ki be-yad khazaqah hotsi'akha YHWH miMitsrayim*. The word *ot* ("sign") upon the hand and *zikkaron* ("memorial") between the eyes -- these are the two phrases that, along with v. 16, Deut. 6:8, and Deut. 11:18, form the basis for the later practice of *tefillin* (phylacteries). Whether the original intent was literal (physical objects bound to hand and forehead) or metaphorical (constant awareness, as in Prov. 3:3; 6:21; 7:3) is debated. The purpose clause -- *lema'an tihyeh torat YHWH be-fikha* ("so that the instruction of YHWH may be in your mouth") -- reveals the goal: hand, forehead, and mouth together. EMET renders *torah* as "torah [instruction]." LOGOS renders "law of the LORD." PNEUMA renders "teaching of YHWH." The consensus renders "instruction of YHWH" for *torat YHWH* -- *torah* here means "instruction, teaching, directive guidance" (not yet the five books of Moses), and "instruction" captures this sense better than "law" (LOGOS, which narrows the semantic range) or "teaching" (PNEUMA, which is acceptable but slightly less formal). LXX: *sēmeion epi tēs cheiros sou kai mnēmosunon pro ophthalmōn sou* ("a sign upon your hand and a memorial before your eyes").

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Mi-yamim yamimah*: "from year to year"** -- Hebrew: *ve-shamarta et-ha-khuqqah ha-zot le-mo'adah mi-yamim yamimah*. The phrase *mi-yamim yamimah* literally means "from days to days" but idiomatically denotes annual recurrence (cf. Judg. 11:40; 21:19; 1 Sam. 1:3). EMET renders "from days to days [mi-yamim yamimah]." LOGOS renders "from year to year." PNEUMA renders "year after year." The consensus follows LOGOS: "from year to year" -- the standard English idiom for annual repetition, clearer than EMET's literal rendering while less casual than PNEUMA's. LXX: *aph' hēmerōn eis hēmeras* ("from days to days").

> **[fn11] v.11 -- Entry formula: "when YHWH brings you"** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah ki-yevi'akha YHWH el-erets ha-Kena'ani ka-asher nishba lekha ve-la'avotekha u-netanah lakh*. This verse echoes v. 5 with the future-entry formula *ve-hayah ki* ("and it shall be when..."). Here only the Canaanite is named, representing the totality of the land's inhabitants as an umbrella term. All three translators agree on the substance. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ve-ha'avarta*: "you shall set apart" and *sheger*: "offspring"** -- Hebrew: *ve-ha'avarta khol-peter-rechem laYHWH ve-khol-peter sheger behemah asher yihyeh lekha ha-zekharim laYHWH*. The verb *ha'avarta* (Hiphil of *avar*, "to pass over, transfer") means "you shall cause to pass over" -- transferring ownership to YHWH. The same root *avar* that described God "passing through" Egypt (12:12) now describes the transfer of the firstborn to God. EMET renders "you shall cause to pass over" (maximally literal). LOGOS renders "you shall set apart." PNEUMA renders "you are to give over." The consensus follows LOGOS: "you shall set apart" -- capturing the transfer-of-ownership meaning without the potential confusion of "pass over" (which readers might associate with the Passover event itself). The rare noun *sheger* ("offspring, issue") is rendered "offspring" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) rather than EMET's glossed transliteration. LXX: *aphorieis pan dianoigon mētran* ("you shall set apart every one opening a womb").

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Tifdeh* and *va'arafto*: "you shall redeem" and "you shall break its neck"** -- Hebrew: *ve-khol-peter chamor tifdeh ve-seh ve-im-lo tifdeh va'arafto ve-khol bekhor adam be-vanekha tifdeh*. The verb *tifdeh* (from *padah*, "to redeem, ransom") appears three times in this verse -- a triple redemption. The donkey, as an unclean animal, cannot be sacrificed; it must be redeemed by substitution of a *seh* ("lamb") or destroyed by neck-breaking (*va'arafto*, from *araf*, "to break the neck"). The human firstborn must likewise be redeemed -- no option of sacrifice exists. This is the theological foundation of *pidyon ha-ben* ("redemption of the firstborn son"). All three translators agree on "redeem" for *tifdeh* and "break its neck" for *va'arafto*. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering. LXX renders the donkey redemption with *allaxeis probatō* ("you shall exchange for a sheep"), using exchange language rather than redemption -- a notable interpretive shift.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Mah-zot*: "What is this?" -- the child's question** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah ki-yish'alkha vinkha makhar lemor mah-zot ve-amarta elav be-khozeq yad hotsi'anu YHWH miMitsrayim mibbeit avadim*. The child's question *mah-zot* ("what is this?") is simpler than the question in 12:26 (*mah ha'avodah hazzot lakhem*, "what is this service to you?"). In rabbinic tradition, this becomes the question of the "simple son" in the four-sons typology of the Haggadah. The word *makhar* ("tomorrow") denotes future generations. EMET renders "tomorrow [makhar]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "tomorrow." PNEUMA renders "in the future." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "tomorrow" -- the standard rendering of *makhar*, which carries the force of "in the future" through its context without paraphrase. LXX reads *meta tauta* ("after these things") for *makhar*.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Hiqshah*: "was stubborn" -- hardening verb** -- Hebrew: *vayehi ki-hiqshah Par'oh le-shallkhenu vayaharog YHWH kol-bekhor be-erets Mitsrayim mibekhor adam ve'ad-bekhor behemah al-ken ani zoveakh laYHWH kol-peter rekhem ha-zekharim ve-khol-bekhor banai efdeh*. The verb *hiqshah* (Hiphil of *qashah*, "to be hard") is the third hardening root alongside *chazaq* and *kaved*. Here it is used retrospectively and intransitively -- Pharaoh "was stubborn" without a specified object (the heart is implied). In the pedagogical context of answering a child's question, Pharaoh's agency is emphasized rather than the complex divine-human interplay of chapters 7-11. EMET renders "made hard [hiqshah] to send us out." LOGOS renders "was stubborn about sending us away." PNEUMA renders "stubbornly refused to let us go." The consensus follows LOGOS: "was stubborn about sending us away" -- capturing the intransitive Hiphil sense while remaining clear. EMET's "made hard" is too literal for English; PNEUMA's "refused to let us go" adds interpretive specificity not in the Hebrew. LXX: *esklērunen Pharaō* ("Pharaoh hardened").

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Totafot*: "frontlets" -- the tefillin connection** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah le'ot al-yadkhah u-le-totafot bein einekha ki be-khozeq yad hotsi'anu YHWH miMitsrayim*. The word *totafot* is one of the most obscure in the Hebrew Bible, occurring only here, Deut. 6:8, and Deut. 11:18. Its etymology is uncertain -- proposed derivations include a root meaning "to bind" or "to encircle," or an Akkadian/Aramaic cognate for "band" or "headband." Rabbinic tradition identifies *totafot* with *tefillin* (phylacteries). In v. 9 the parallel term was *zikkaron* ("memorial"); here it is *totafot* -- shifting from abstract reminder to concrete physical marker. EMET renders "totafot [frontlets]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "frontlets." PNEUMA renders "emblem." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "frontlets" -- the traditional rendering that captures the positional meaning (something worn on the front of the head) without committing to a specific etymology. PNEUMA's "emblem" is too generic and loses the forehead-specific connotation. LXX: *asaleuton pro ophthalmōn sou* ("immovable before your eyes") -- a remarkable rendering suggesting permanence.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Derekh erets Pelishtim* and the n-ch-m wordplay** -- Hebrew: *vayehi be-shallakh Par'oh et-ha'am velo-nakham Elohim derekh erets Pelishtim ki qarov hu ki amar Elohim pen-yinnakhem ha'am bir'otam milkhamah veshavu Mitsraymah*. A remarkable wordplay on the root n-ch-m: *velo-nakham* ("and he did not lead them") uses *nakhah* ("to lead, guide"), while *pen-yinnakhem* ("lest they change their minds") uses the Niphal of *nakham* ("to be sorry, relent"). The *derekh erets Pelishtim* ("way of the land of the Philistines") was the coastal highway -- later the Via Maris -- the most direct route to Canaan, heavily garrisoned with military outposts. God's reasoning is explicitly psychological: newly freed slaves would flee from war and return to Egypt. All three translators agree on the core rendering. PNEUMA paraphrases "even though that was the shorter route" for *ki qarov hu* ("though it was near"). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "though it was near" -- preserving the Hebrew's concise *qarov* without explanatory expansion. LXX: *ouch hōdēgēsen autous ho theos hodon gēs Phulistiim* ("God did not lead them by the road of the land of the Philistines").

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Chamushim*: "armed" and *Yam-Suf*: "Sea of Reeds"** -- Hebrew: *vayassev Elohim et-ha'am derekh ha-midbar Yam-Suf va-chamushim alu venei-Yisra'el me-erets Mitsrayim*. The verb *vayassev* (Hiphil of *savav*, "to turn, go around") indicates a deliberate detour. **Key decision on *chamushim*:** This is one of the most debated words in Exodus. Three main interpretations: (a) "armed, equipped for battle" -- from a root *ch-m-sh* related to military equipment, supported by the word's use in Josh. 1:14; 4:12; Judg. 7:11; (b) "in ranks of five, in orderly formation" -- from *chamesh* ("five"); (c) "in the fifth generation" -- the LXX reading (*pemptē de genea*). The military interpretation has the strongest contextual support: v. 17 mentions the possibility of "war" (*milkhamah*), and *alu* ("went up") often denotes military ascent. The consensus renders "armed" -- following the dominant Hebrew interpretation and the military context. The LXX's "in the fifth generation" reflects a different but internally coherent tradition. **On *Yam-Suf*:** The Hebrew *suf* means "reed, rush" (cf. Exod. 2:3, 5). The traditional "Red Sea" derives from the LXX's *erythra thalassa*. The consensus renders "Sea of Reeds" per SOPHIA convention, reflecting the Hebrew etymology. LXX: *pemptē de genea anebēsan hoi huioi Israēl ek gēs Aiguptou* ("in the fifth generation the sons of Israel went up from the land of Egypt").

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Hashbea hishbia* and *paqod yifqod*: the double emphatics** -- Hebrew: *vayiqqakh Mosheh et-atsmot Yosef immo ki hashbea hishbia et-benei Yisra'el lemor paqod yifqod Elohim etkhem ve-ha'alitem et-atsmotai mizzeh ittkhem*. Two infinitive absolute constructions: *hashbea hishbia* ("he made solemnly swear" -- Hiphil infinitive absolute + Hiphil perfect of *shava*, "to swear") and *paqod yifqod* ("will surely visit" -- Qal infinitive absolute + Qal imperfect of *paqad*, "to visit, attend to"). Both are emphatic: the oath was maximally binding, and the divine visitation was absolutely certain. This verse fulfills Gen. 50:25. The bones of Joseph are the physical thread connecting the patriarchal promise to the exodus fulfillment -- they will not be buried until Josh. 24:32 at Shechem. EMET renders "caused to swear [hashbea hishbia]" and "visiting, God will visit [paqod yifqod]." LOGOS renders "solemnly swear" and "surely visit." PNEUMA renders "bound by a solemn oath" and "surely come to your aid." The consensus follows LOGOS: "made the sons of Israel solemnly swear" and "God will surely visit you" -- "solemnly swear" captures the intensification of the infinitive absolute, and "surely visit" preserves the theological verb *paqad* ("to visit") that denotes divine intervention. PNEUMA's "come to your aid" interprets rather than translates *paqad*. LXX: *horkō gar hōrkisen... episkopē episkesetai humas kurios* ("for with an oath he made swear... with a visitation the Lord will visit you").

> **[fn20] v.20 -- Succoth to Etham** -- Hebrew: *vayis'u miSukkot vayakhanu be'Etam bi-qtseh ha-midbar*. The second encampment after Rameses. *Sukkot* ("booths") was the first stop (12:37). *Etam* stands at the *qatseh* ("edge, extremity") of the wilderness -- the threshold between inhabited land and the open desert. All three translators agree on the substance. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ammud anan* and *ammud esh*: "pillar of cloud" and "pillar of fire"** -- Hebrew: *va-YHWH holekh lifneihem yomam be'ammud anan lanakhotam ha-derekh ve-lailah be'ammud esh le-ha'ir lahem lalekhet yomam va-lailah*. This is the first appearance of the *ammud anan* ("pillar of cloud") and *ammud esh* ("pillar of fire") -- the visible manifestation of YHWH's guidance. The noun *ammud* ("pillar, column") from *amad* ("to stand") denotes a vertical standing structure. The cloud guides by day (*lanakhotam ha-derekh*, "to lead them on the way," from *nakhah*, the same root as in v. 17); the fire illuminates by night (*le-ha'ir lahem*, "to give light to them"). The participle *holekh* ("walking, going") depicts YHWH as personally marching at the head of the column -- an anthropomorphic image of divine companionship. All three translators agree on the core rendering. PNEUMA restructures the syntax but preserves all elements. The LXX reads *ho theos* ("God") where the MT reads YHWH -- a notable substitution in the Vaticanus manuscript. LXX: *ho de theos hēgeito autōn hēmeras men en stulō nephelēs... tēn de nukta en stulō puros* ("and God was leading them by day in a pillar of cloud... and by night in a pillar of fire").

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Lo-yamish*: "He did not remove"** -- Hebrew: *lo-yamish ammud he'anan yomam ve'ammud ha'esh lailah lifnei ha'am*. The verb *yamish* (Hiphil of *mush*, "to depart, remove") -- negated: "He did not remove." The implied subject is YHWH (from v. 21). The definite articles *he'anan* ("the cloud") and *ha'esh* ("the fire") refer back to the pillars just introduced. The emphatic negative affirms constant, unbroken divine guidance -- the pillar never withdrew. EMET renders "he did not remove [lo-yamish]." LOGOS renders "he did not remove." PNEUMA renders "never left its place in front of the people." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "He did not remove" -- preserving the Hebrew's verbal structure with YHWH as implied agent, rather than PNEUMA's restructuring that makes the pillar the subject. LXX: *ouk exelipen ho stulos tēs nephelēs* ("the pillar of cloud did not fail") -- LXX makes the pillar the subject, not YHWH.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. *Peter Kol-Rechem* (v. 2) -- "The Opener of Every Womb"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the opener of every rechem [womb]" | Literal: transliteration of *rechem* |
| LOGOS | "the opener of every womb" | Formal: full English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the first to open every womb" | Dynamic: adjectival rephrasing |

The consensus renders "the opener of every womb." The Hebrew *peter* is a noun ("opener"), not an adjective ("first to open"). EMET and LOGOS preserve the nominal form; PNEUMA converts it to an adjectival phrase. The noun *peter* carries the physical force of "the one who breaks open" -- the first issue that opens the birth canal. The consensus preserves this substantive form while rendering *rechem* in English rather than transliterating.

### 2. *Ot* / *Zikkaron* (v. 9) -- "Sign" and "Memorial"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sign [le-ot]... remembrance [le-zikkaron]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "sign [le'ot]... memorial [ulezikkaron]" | Formal with transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "sign... reminder" | Dynamic: simplified |

The consensus renders "sign" for *ot* and "memorial" for *zikkaron*. PNEUMA's "reminder" weakens *zikkaron*, which carries the weight of a formal commemorative object (cf. 12:14, where *zikkaron* describes the Passover day itself). "Memorial" preserves the solemnity of the term and its connection to the *zakhar* ("remember") imperative of v. 3.

### 3. *Tifdeh* (v. 13) -- "You Shall Redeem"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall redeem [tifdeh]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "you shall redeem [tifdeh]" | Formal with transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "you must redeem" | Dynamic: "must" for obligation |

All three translators agree on "redeem" for *padah*. The minor disagreement is on modal: "shall" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "must" (PNEUMA). The consensus follows "shall" -- the standard rendering for Hebrew imperfect in legal contexts, consistent with SOPHIA convention throughout Exodus.

### 4. *Totafot* (v. 16) -- "Frontlets"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "totafot [frontlets]" | Literal: transliteration with gloss |
| LOGOS | "frontlets [totafot]" | Formal: English with transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "emblem" | Dynamic: interpretive |

The consensus renders "frontlets." This word of uncertain etymology (occurring only three times in the Hebrew Bible: here, Deut. 6:8, Deut. 11:18) is traditionally rendered "frontlets" -- capturing the positional meaning (something on the forehead) without committing to a contested etymology. PNEUMA's "emblem" is too generic and loses the forehead-specific reference. Transliteration as "totafot" was considered but rejected in favor of the accessible traditional rendering, consistent with SOPHIA convention of rendering in English with notes.

### 5. *Derekh Erets Pelishtim* (v. 17) -- "The Way of the Land of the Philistines"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the way of the land of the Pelishtim" | Literal: transliterated name |
| LOGOS | "way of the land of the Philistines" | Formal: standard name |
| PNEUMA | "the road through the land of the Philistines" | Dynamic: "through" for clarity |

The consensus renders "the way of the land of the Philistines" -- following the Hebrew construct chain *derekh erets Pelishtim* precisely. PNEUMA's "through" is an interpretive addition; the Hebrew uses a construct chain ("way of the land of") without a preposition. Standard English name "Philistines" follows SOPHIA convention.

### 6. *Chamushim* (v. 18) -- "Armed"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in battle-array / armed / in fifths" | Literal: triple gloss |
| LOGOS | "armed" | Formal: dominant interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "organized for battle" | Dynamic: paraphrase |

The consensus renders "armed" -- following the dominant Hebrew interpretation. The military context (v. 17 mentions *milkhamah*, "war"; *alu*, "went up," is military language) strongly supports the martial reading. The LXX's "in the fifth generation" (*pemptē genea*) reflects a genealogical interpretation that, while coherent, lacks the contextual support of the military reading. PNEUMA's "organized for battle" is a reasonable paraphrase but adds specificity not in the Hebrew. EMET's triple gloss, while thorough, is unsuitable for a consensus translation text.

### 7. *Paqod Yifqod* (v. 19) -- "Will Surely Visit"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "visiting, God will visit [paqod yifqod]" | Literal: preserves infinitive absolute pattern |
| LOGOS | "will surely visit [paqod yifqod]" | Formal: English intensifier |
| PNEUMA | "will surely come to your aid" | Dynamic: interprets *paqad* |

The consensus renders "will surely visit" -- following LOGOS. The infinitive absolute construction (*paqod yifqod*) is an emphatic device best rendered in English with "surely" as an intensifier. EMET's "visiting, God will visit" preserves the Hebrew doubling pattern but reads awkwardly in English. PNEUMA's "come to your aid" interprets the theological verb *paqad* rather than translating it. *Paqad* means "to visit, attend to, number, appoint" -- a rich term that should be preserved rather than collapsed into a single interpretation.

### 8. *Ammud Anan* / *Ammud Esh* (vv. 21-22) -- "Pillar of Cloud" / "Pillar of Fire"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pillar of cloud [be-ammud anan]... pillar of fire [be-ammud esh]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "pillar of cloud [be'ammud anan]... pillar of fire [be'ammud esh]" | Formal with transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "pillar of cloud... pillar of fire" | Dynamic: same rendering |

All three translators agree on "pillar of cloud" and "pillar of fire." The consensus follows the unanimous rendering. The noun *ammud* ("pillar, column") from *amad* ("to stand") denotes a vertical standing structure. The cloud and fire are the dual theophanic media of divine guidance -- cloud by day, fire by night -- ensuring uninterrupted divine accompaniment.

### 9. Recurring Convention Disagreements

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (He, His, My); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Mitsrayim); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Egypt). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**e. Transliteration in-text:** EMET embeds Hebrew transliterations throughout the verse text; LOGOS places transliterations in brackets after English; PNEUMA uses English only. SOPHIA follows English text with Hebrew noted in footnotes. (Recurring, LOW)

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 13

| Verse | Hebrew | Verb/Root | Form | Agent | SOPHIA Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13:15 | *hiqshah Par'oh leshallechenu* | *qashah* | Hiphil (intransitive) | Pharaoh (retrospective) | "Pharaoh was stubborn about sending us away" |

*Note: This is the first appearance of the qashah root in a hardening context since 7:3 (where God announced "I will harden [aqsheh] Pharaoh's heart"). Here the verb appears in retrospective summary within the catechetical instruction for the child's question. The Hiphil is intransitive -- Pharaoh "was stubborn" without a specified object (the heart is implied but absent). In the pedagogical context, the emphasis falls on Pharaoh's agency rather than the complex divine-human interplay of chapters 7-11. The next hardening verb will appear in 14:4 (chizzaqti, "I will strengthen," Piel, divine agent), when God hardens Pharaoh's heart for the final pursuit to the Sea of Reeds.*

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 13:1-22 (22 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant (especially the "sixth generation" reading for chamushim at v. 18, the "six days" variant at v. 6, the Gergesites addition at v. 5, the allassō/"exchange" rendering for padah at v. 13, and the ho theos/YHWH substitution at v. 21). One hardening verb in chapter 13: hiqshah (v. 15, qashah Hiphil, Pharaoh's agency). Key renderings: "the opener of every womb" (v. 2, peter kol-rechem), "by strength of hand" (vv. 3, 9, 14, 16 -- the chapter's fourfold refrain), "on account of this" (v. 8, ba'avur zeh), "sign... memorial" (v. 9, ot/zikkaron), "frontlets" (v. 16, totafot), "the way of the land of the Philistines" (v. 17, derekh erets Pelishtim), "armed" (v. 18, chamushim), "Sea of Reeds" (v. 18, Yam-Suf), "will surely visit" (v. 19, paqod yifqod), "pillar of cloud... pillar of fire" (vv. 21-22, ammud anan/ammud esh). The eight key translation decisions identified in the prompt have been adjudicated with full documentation.*

--- SOPHIA
